title: Listening - Part 3: The Quiet Crisis
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a marine biologist, Dr. David Vance, and a shipping industry consultant, Claire Holt, about the problem of underwater noise pollution. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing how human-made noise is affecting marine communication and migration.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We often think of the deep sea as a 'silent world', but that’s far from the truth. David, how has the noise level changed?

David: [15]Since the 1950s, low-frequency noise in the ocean has doubled every decade.[/15] It’s mostly from shipping, sonar, and oil exploration. For whales and dolphins, who use sound to 'see' and communicate, it’s like trying to have a conversation in the middle of a heavy metal concert. Their world is becoming a fog of noise.

Interviewer: Claire, the shipping industry is responsible for much of this. Are companies taking it seriously?

Claire: They are, but [16]the problem is that 'quiet' ships are traditionally less fuel-efficient.[/16] The propeller designs that reduce noise often require more energy to push the ship through water. In an industry with tiny profit margins and rising fuel costs, choosing the 'quiet' option is a hard sell for most CEOs.

Interviewer: David, what’s the actual impact on the animals? Do they just swim away?

David: [17]I was actually quite alarmed to find that some species don't swim away—they just stop singing.[/17] We’ve seen blue whales go silent for days when a seismic survey is nearby. If they aren't communicating, they aren't mating or finding food. It’s a silent disaster because we don't see the bodies—we just see the population decline years later.

Interviewer: Claire, are there any technical solutions that don't hurt the bottom line?

Claire: [18]I believe the real hope lies in 'air lubrication' systems, where bubbles reduce the friction between the hull and the water.[/18] It makes the ship both quieter and more efficient. It’s a win-win, but the retrofitting cost for existing fleets is still the biggest obstacle.

Interviewer: David, you’ve mentioned that the COVID-19 lockdowns provided an accidental experiment. What did we learn?

David: [19]It was an extraordinary 'anthropause'. When shipping stopped, we recorded the lowest stress levels in whales that we’ve ever seen.[/19] It proved that if we give them just a little bit of quiet, their biology responds almost instantly. It was a rare moment of hope.

Interviewer: Finally, what’s the most urgent step?

Claire: [20]We need international regulations that set a 'noise limit' for all new commercial vessels.[/20] Voluntary measures aren't enough. David and I might come at this from different angles—science and business—but we both know that a noisy ocean is a dying ocean.

questions.question: What does David say about the increase in underwater noise?
options: A) It has stayed relatively constant since the 1950s.
B) It is mostly caused by natural heavy metal deposits.
C) It has doubled every ten years for several decades.
D) It is only a problem for whales that live near oil rigs.
questions.explanation: David states that 'since the 1950s, low-frequency noise in the ocean has doubled every decade'.

questions.question: According to Claire, why do shipping companies avoid quiet propellers?
options: A) They are too difficult to manufacture for modern ships.
B) They tend to increase the amount of fuel a ship consumes.
C) They have very large profit margins that CEOs dislike.
D) They are not compatible with oil exploration sonar.
questions.explanation: She explains that 'quiet' ships are 'traditionally less fuel-efficient'.

questions.question: David was surprised to discover that some marine animals respond to noise by
options: A) swimming away to find new seismic surveys.
B) singing more loudly to overcome the background fog.
C) ceasing their vocal communication entirely.
D) finding food more quickly than they did before.
questions.explanation: He was alarmed to find that some species 'just stop singing' when noise is present.

questions.question: What is the benefit of the 'air lubrication' systems mentioned by Claire?
options: A) They make existing fleets much cheaper to retrofit.
B) They eliminate the need for any shipping regulations.
C) They improve both noise levels and energy efficiency.
D) They prevent friction between the bubbles and the hull.
questions.explanation: She says it makes the ship 'both quieter and more efficient', calling it a 'win-win'.

questions.question: The 'anthropause' during COVID-19 lockdowns showed that
options: A) whale stress levels are not affected by human activity.
B) shipping must stop completely for whales to survive.
C) marine life can recover quickly when noise is reduced.
D) recorded noise levels are higher when ships are stopped.
questions.explanation: David says it proved that 'if we give them just a little bit of quiet, their biology responds almost instantly'.

questions.question: Claire believes that the most effective way to solve the problem is through
options: A) voluntary measures taken by individual companies.
B) mandatory global standards for ship noise.
C) a complete ban on all commercial vessels.
D) letting scientists and businesses work from different angles.
questions.explanation: She states, 'We need international regulations that set a noise limit... Voluntary measures aren't enough'.
